title: Utility-Based Agents
concept: Agents that score multiple paths based on a cost/efficiency utility function before acting.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what makes a goal-based agent special?

options.text: It keeps an explicit goal and checks if actions bring it closer
It only reacts to what it sees right now, with no goal
It picks random actions and hopes for the best

exercises.explain: A goal-based agent holds a clear picture of the state it wants to reach, then judges each possible action by whether it moves closer to that goal. That's the foundation we now build on.

cards.title: Which road is BEST?
cards.body: A goal-based agent just wants to reach the airport. But three routes all get there! One is fast, one is cheap on gas, one avoids traffic. How does it choose?

cards.title: Utility-Based Agent
cards.body: A utility-based agent gives each possible path a score for how 'good' it is, not just whether it works. It then picks the path with the highest score.

cards.title: Smart Shopper
cards.body: Imagine buying cereal. A goal-based shopper grabs any box that is cereal. A utility-based shopper compares price, taste, and health, then picks the best overall deal.

cards.title: The GPS App
cards.body: Your maps app finds many routes to home. It scores each by time, distance, and traffic, then highlights the one with the best total score. That score is the utility function.

cards.title: Myth: More goals reached = better
cards.body: Reaching the goal isn't enough for a utility agent. Two paths both succeed, but it still prefers the cheaper, faster one. It cares about HOW WELL, not just IF.

cards.title: Goal vs. Utility
cards.body: Both agents reach the goal, but they decide differently along the way.

left.text: Asks: 'Does this action reach the goal?' Yes or no.

right.text: Asks: 'How good is this path?' Scores and ranks them.

exercises.prompt: What does a utility-based agent do that a plain goal-based agent does not?

options.text: It scores multiple successful paths and picks the best-scoring one
It always chooses the first path it finds
It refuses to act until a human helps

exercises.explain: The key upgrade is the utility function: it rates each path (cost, speed, safety) and chooses the highest score, instead of accepting any path that merely works.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A utility-based agent only checks whether a path reaches the goal, and never compares how good the paths are.
exercises.explain: False! Comparing how good each path is (its utility score) is exactly what makes it utility-based. That comparison is its main job.

exercises.sentence: A utility-based agent gives each path a score, then picks the one with the highest score.
exercises.explain: The word 'score' captures the whole idea: utility means putting a number on how good each option is, so the agent can compare and choose the best.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence sounds like it was written by an AI describing a utility-based agent?

options.text: A utility-based agent evaluates candidate paths against a utility function, then selects the option that maximizes overall expected value.
um it just kinda picks the road that feels okay i guess lol
My teacher said pick fast route home, so that one, done.

exercises.explain: Option A uses smooth, precise, textbook phrasing like 'candidate paths' and 'maximizes overall expected value' — a giveaway of AI-generated writing, while the others sound casual and human.

exercises.prompt: A delivery drone must reach a house. Route 1 is fast but risky in wind. Route 2 is slow but safe. Route 3 is medium speed and safe. A utility-based agent scores by speed AND safety together. Which route is it MOST likely to choose, and why?

options.text: Route 3, because scoring speed and safety together gives it the best overall balance
Route 1, because reaching the goal fastest is the only thing that matters
Route 2, because a utility agent always avoids all risk no matter the cost
None, because a utility agent can't compare paths

exercises.explain: A utility-based agent doesn't chase a single factor — it blends multiple factors (like speed and safety) into one score and picks the highest total. Route 3 balances both, so it usually wins. That's the whole point: choosing the BEST path, not just A working path.
